name: Alcala del Rio from Seville – circular via La Algaba and La Rinconada
name_original: Alcalá del Río desde Sevilla – circular por La Algaba y La Rinconada
region_intro: No traffic touring cycling routes around the Guadalquivir traverse a diverse landscape shaped by the river basin, ranging from expansive marshlands and fertile agricultural plains to the more rugged, mountainous terrain of the Sierra Morena. The region features numerous riverside paths and dedicated greenways, often utilizing former railway lines, providing flat and accessible cycling. Further inland, the landscape transitions to rolling hills and olive groves, with some routes ascending into the higher elevations of the Sierra Morena, offering more challenging climbs and reservoir views. Best no traffic touring cycling routes around the Guadalquivir The most popular no traffic touring cycling route is Almódovar del Río from Córdoba — circular via the Guadalmellato canal service lane, a 41.4 miles (66.7 km) trail that takes 5 hours 30 minutes to complete, offering a challenging ride along a canal service lane. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Mozarabic Way and Ategua Countryside Loop, a difficult 53.3 miles (85.7 km) path that explores historical routes and rural landscapes. Local touring cyclists also love the Route of the Reservoirs: Guadalmellato Loop, a 28.4 miles (45.8 km) trail leading through the Sierra Morena, often completed in about 3 hours 23 minutes. Touring cycling around the Guadalquivir is defined by riverside greenways, agricultural plains, and the foothills of the Sierra Morena, with routes catering to various fitness levels. The network includes easy, moderate, and difficult options. The routes in Guadalquivir are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 600 reviews. More than 11,000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadalquivir's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no traffic touring cycling routes are available in the Guadalquivir region?
A: There are over 225 dedicated no traffic touring cycling routes around the Guadalquivir. These routes cater to various skill levels, with 22 easy, 80 moderate, and 123 difficult options available for exploration.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in the Guadalquivir region?
A: The Guadalquivir region generally enjoys pleasant weather, making spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) ideal for touring cycling. During these seasons, temperatures are mild, and the landscapes are vibrant, offering comfortable riding conditions. Summers can be very hot, especially inland, while winters are generally mild but can have cooler, wetter periods.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see along the no traffic touring cycling routes?
A: The routes offer a diverse range of natural beauty. You can cycle through the vast &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guadalquivir_Marshes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalquivir Marshes&lt;/a&gt;, experience the critical wetland ecosystems near Doñana National Park, or explore the more mountainous terrain of the Sierra Morena. Specific natural highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3168204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite&lt;/a&gt; recreational area with its natural pool, and the panoramic views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3896738" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Isla Cabeza de la Viña Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; in the Tranco reservoir.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or cultural sites to visit on these routes?
A: Absolutely. Many routes pass through or near significant historical and cultural sites. In Seville, you can see iconic landmarks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/215404" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seville Cathedral&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5343886" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tower of Gold&lt;/a&gt;. Further inland, Córdoba boasts the magnificent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4041503" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mosque–Cathedral of Córdoba&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features Roman vestiges, including the ancient Via Augusta and archaeological sites like Itálica in Santiponce, the birthplace of Roman emperors.

Q: Are there family-friendly no traffic touring cycling routes in the Guadalquivir region?
A: Yes, the region offers several easy, traffic-free routes perfect for families. Many greenways (vías verdes) and riverside paths provide flat, well-maintained surfaces away from vehicle traffic. These routes often connect charming towns and offer opportunities for leisurely rides suitable for all ages.

Q: What are the surface conditions like on these no traffic touring cycling routes?
A: The surface conditions vary depending on the route. Many greenways and urban paths offer smooth, paved surfaces ideal for touring bikes. However, routes venturing into natural parks like the Sierra Morena, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026466407/route-of-the-reservoirs-guadalmellato-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalmellato Loop&lt;/a&gt;, may include gravel sections or unpaved tracks. It's always recommended to check the specific route details on komoot for surface information.

Q: Can I find circular no traffic touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, there are many excellent circular routes designed for touring cyclists. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026385540/almodovar-del-rio-from-cordoba-circular-via-the-guadalmellato-canal-service-lane" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almódovar del Río from Córdoba — circular via the Guadalmellato canal service lane&lt;/a&gt; offers a challenging loop with significant elevation changes. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996362010/ermita-de-san-gines-via-camino-viejo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita de San Ginés via Camino Viejo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, providing a scenic circular experience.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in the Guadalquivir region?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.37 stars. Reviewers often praise the region's diverse landscapes, from tranquil riverside paths to challenging mountain trails, and the opportunity to combine cycling with exploring rich historical and cultural sites. The extensive network of greenways and traffic-free options is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there options for public transport access to the starting points of these routes?
A: Many towns and cities along the Guadalquivir, such as Seville and Córdoba, are well-connected by public transport, including trains and buses, which can often accommodate bicycles or are close to route starting points. It's advisable to check local transport provider policies regarding bicycle carriage in advance.

Q: Are there any less crowded or 'hidden gem' no traffic touring cycling routes?
A: While popular routes attract more visitors, the vast network of greenways and rural paths means it's easy to find quieter options. Exploring routes through the agricultural landscapes, such as those in La Vega or the expansive olive groves, can offer a serene and less crowded touring experience away from the main tourist hubs.

Q: What kind of wildlife and flora can I observe on the cycle trails?
A: The Guadalquivir basin is rich in biodiversity. Cycling near the &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guadalquivir_Marshes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalquivir Marshes&lt;/a&gt; and Doñana National Park offers exceptional birdwatching opportunities, including significant populations of migratory birds, and a chance to spot rare species like the Iberian lynx and Spanish imperial eagles. Inland, you'll encounter extensive olive groves, cereal crops, and diverse Mediterranean flora in the mountainous areas.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay along the no traffic touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes pass through or near charming towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. Cities like Seville and Córdoba offer a wide range of options. Even in smaller towns like Montoro, you'll discover local eateries and places to rest, allowing for comfortable multi-day touring.
tips: The green lung of the city of Seville, with an area of 120 hectares after its expansion in 2014. The entire Isla de la Cartuja was undeveloped until Expo '92 - the area was recovered for the city on the occasion of the celebration and for acclimatize the plants that arrived from all over the planet destined for the sample.

The monument "Nacimiento de Un Hombre Nuevo" pays tribute to the famous sailor CHRISTOPH COLUMBUS (born around 1451, died 1506). He undertook his great voyages of discovery, which make him famous to this day - from the European mainland across the Atlantic to the west and looking for the sea route to India - on behalf of the Spanish crown, but he was born in Genoa (Italy).
However, the name of the huge double continent that he discovered in 1492 (without even suspecting this) was borrowed from another explorer: his contemporary AMERIGO VESPUCCI became the namesake for America.

The Puente del Alamillo is an impressive cable-stayed bridge in Seville, Spain, designed by renowned architect Santiago Calatrava. The bridge was built for the Expo '92 and spans the Guadalquivir River. It is a unique cantilever spar cable-stayed bridge without back supports, with a height of 140 metres and a span of 200 metres.
